journal-meta.journal-id: Int J Qual Stud Health Well-being
Int J Qual Stud Health Well-being

journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Qualitative Studies on Health and Well-being

title-group.article-title: Parental alienation and its impact on self-esteem and well-being - a meta-Ethnography
title-group.alt-title: I. Ø. RØYSLAND AND C. A.-L. KOSKINEN
INTERNATIONAL JOURNAL OF QUALITATIVE STUDIES ON HEALTH AND WELL-BEING

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Public Health, Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Stavanger, Stavanger, Norway
bDepartment of Care and Ethics, Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Stavanger, Stavanger, Norway

author-notes.corresp: *CONTACT Ingrid Ølfarnes Røysland ingrid.roysland@uis.no Department of Public Health, Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Stavanger, N-4036, Stavanger, Norway

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Previous studies have highlighted that the transformations of the family of origin and parent-child relationships impact children's personal growth and mental health, which persist well into adulthood. Research has mainly focused on experiences of parental divorce during childhood, which motivated us to examine what impact parental alienation during childhood has on self-esteem, and well-being in adulthood.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Original qualitative studies were identified and systematically synthesised using a meta-ethnographic approach. Six databases were searched for relevant full-text articles in English, as well as in Danish, Norwegian, and Swedish. There were no limitations concerning the year of publication. Articles were first screened against inclusion criteria for eligibility and then assessed for quality and analysed using Noblit and Hare’s seven-step meta-ethnography process. The ENTREQ checklist for systematic reviews was used. Ten qualitative studies were included in the analysis.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The analysis resulted in four themes: Lack of trust and insecure relationships, Emotional pain, feelings of loss and guilt, Low self-esteem affects life paths and Embracing strategies and changes. A synthesis of the four themes is illuminated in the line of argument and metaphor: A tree that grows in the shadow of its roots.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: By understanding the experiences parental alienation has on adults’ self-esteem and wellbeing, health and social care professionals, services, and educational institutions can better support and help persons to find the strength in their roots, find a balanced sense of vulnerability and freedom that contributes to health and well-being, and an ability to follow their own life path.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: In recent decades, there has been an increase in the number of divorcees and separations (Goldscheider et al., 2015; Härkönen et al., 2017; Maslauskaitė, 2017; Mortelmans, 2020). As a result, an increasing number of children and adolescents have experienced changes in their parental family structure. Previous research highlights the risk that previous spouses who share parental responsibility stay in conflict after their separation (Darwiche et al., 2022), and that disputes may even result in situations of parental alienation. Thuen et al. (2021) study shows that in Nordic countries, 12% of the adolescents by the age of 17 years reported loss of parental contact, mostly with fathers.

Parental alienation or estrangement occurs when one member of a family voluntarily and intentionally distances themselves from other family members in an attempt to sever relational ties or create boundaries that limit interaction (Scharp & Thomas, 2016). There is no generally accepted definition of parental alienation; however, Gardner (1992) defines parental alienation as the estrangement of a child from one parent by the other parent, who applies unfounded criticism of the estranged parent. According to Meland et al., (2023), parental alienation is recognised among parents as a form of harmful behaviour where both mothers and fathers suffer. The unwanted denigration toward the targeted parent is, according to Gardner (1992), also an essential component of the development of parental alienation syndrome in the child. Previous qualitative studies have examined parental alienation syndrome, which can lead to severe emotional, psychological, social, and legal consequences (Kucukkargagoz & Meylani, 2025). Previous studies show that parental alienation can be harmful to children’s health and functioning, and children and adolescents who grow up with an absent or alienated parent run a greater risk of uncertainty when it comes to progress in school and education, interpersonal relationships, crime, mental health obstacles, and their parenting role (Gerrand & Nduna, 2021; Lundberg, 2017; Meland et al., 2023; Reuven‐Krispin et al., 2021).

Young adulthood is a crucial stage of life, personal growth, education, work, interpersonal relationships, parenting, and the development of one’s self-esteem. Previous research suggests that the circumstances of the family of origin and the parent-child relationships can have consequences for personal growth and mental health that persist well into adulthood (Amato & Sobolewski, 2001; Portilla-Saavedra et al., 2023; Reuven‐Krispin et al., 2021; Sobolewski & Amato, 2007). Summing up, we have found that the implications of parental divorcee for children and adolescents’ outcomes have been widely studied (Amato & Anthony, 2014; Cavanagh, 2008; Härkönen et al., 2017; McLanahan et al., 2013; Schneider et al., 2018), but less attention has been given to the experiences of young adults (20+) who have undergone parental alienation during childhood.

There is a need for an extended understanding of the impact parental alienation during childhood has on adulthood and adults’ lives, self-esteem, and well-being. Previous research sparked an interest in deepening the understanding of young adults’ lives and well-being, and their ability to navigate through life. The objective of the study is to gain a deeper understanding of the potential long-term consequences of parental alienation on adult life and contribute to the development of healthcare practices.

sec.title: Theoretical basis and central concepts
sec.p: Galvin (2018) defines self-esteem or identity as a dynamic and constantly changing process. Human beings are always in a process of becoming; humans are not locked into a certain identity but can change and shape ourselves through our experiences and actions. According to Galvin and Todres (2013, 2011) well-being embraces the bodily well-being and the experience of physical health and comfort, social well-being, and the feeling of having supportive and meaningful relationships, emotional well-being, emotional balance, and the ability to manage emotions, existential well-being and the feeling of having a meaningful and coherent life story and spiritual well-being and the experience of having a deeper meaning and purpose in life. These forms of well-being are not isolated; they are resources that are intertwined and interrelated and contribute to an overall experience of well-being.

sec.title: Aim
sec.p: The aim is to examine the impact that parental alienation during childhood has on self-esteem and well-being in adulthood. The meta-synthesis addresses the following research question: What are the experiences of parental alienation during childhood for adults’ lives, self-esteem, becoming, and well-being?

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A meta-synthesis approach based on the interpretative meta-ethnography method described by Noblit and Hare (1988) is adopted in this review. The main idea of a meta-synthesis is to interpret and integrate the qualitative research findings of a particular phenomenon with consideration of variations (Bondas, 2025; Bondas & Hall, 2007). The enhancement of transparency in this synthesis of qualitative research (ENTREQ) is followed in reporting in this meta-ethnographic study (Tong et al., 2012). Meta-synthesis is an interpretive act, and therefore, the researchers’ backgrounds and perspectives inevitably shape the synthesis. The researchers' backgrounds in this study cover affiliation in health sciences, caring science, and nursing science. The researchers have extensive experience in research and practice in healthcare, mental health, and well-being, and in the use of several interpretive research methods. The researchers have been aware of their preconceptions and have consistently reflected on how to rein in and challenge their pre-understanding.

According to Noblit and Hare (1988), Phase I in the meta-synthesis is to get started and review previous research. This initial exploration revealed that parental alienation and its impact on adults’ identity and well-being have been addressed to some extent in existing studies; however, the topic remains under-recognised and warrants further scholarly awareness.

Phase II involved determining further search strategies. Inclusion and exclusion criteria were established through collaborative discussion, ensuring the selection of studies aligned with the research focus.

The search strategy consisted of the search terms as shown in Table I. Search terms were decided to limit the results by study design, and selected search terms related to qualitative research were also added. The MESH database was used to find the search terms.

tr.td: Young adult* (adult/adults/adulthood)Young menYoung womenYoung pe* (people/persons)Early adj3 adulthood (early adulthood, early in adulthood, early stages of adulthood)Emerging adult* (adult/adults/adulthood)College student*University student*Undergraduate*

Child* (childhood, child, children, child’s, children’s)Young ageEarl* age (early age, earlier age)Earl* adj2 life (early life, early in life, earlier in life)Infan* (infant, infants, infancy)Toddler*Minor*School* (schoolchild, schoolchildren, schoolage, schoolaged)Minor*Youngster*Teen* (teen, teens, teenager, teenagers)Adolescen* (adolescent, adolescents, adolescence)Youth*Juvenile*

sec.p: The inclusion criteria were peer-reviewed empirical qualitative studies that focused on the experiences of parental alienation during childhood for young adults. No restrictions were applied to the years searched and the scientific discipline, but the papers included were limited to English, Danish, Norwegian, and Swedish, and publications in online peer-reviewed journals. Studies involving mixed-method designs, quantitative methods, meta-syntheses, and systematic reviews were excluded. Initial searches resulted in a total of 3230 results. The search results were exported to EndNote, where they were duplicated (N = 1700).

The following databases were searched, with the final searches performed in April 2025: Academic Search Ultimate (Ebsco), Cinahl (Ebsco), Medline (Ebsco), APA PsychINFO, Scopus, and Web of Science Core Collection. The first author and the librarian searched the literature together, and the second author searched separately. Both authors worked together to further identify eligible studies based on the criteria after individual searches were performed. The search strategy is described in Appendix A (https://doi.org/10.18710/SY8T6Q).

The title and abstract were read of all the screened records. It was a total of 1530. The first selection was on the background of this reading. Then full-text versions were read. If the content was adequate in relation to the aim of the present study, they were included. Finally, full-text versions of the remaining articles were read and reread. Three studies were excluded for wrong sample and two for wrong method. Only ten of the 3230 original articles (including duplicates) met the inclusion criteria. To illustrate the inclusion process of relevant studies, a PRISMA flow diagram was developed (Figure 1).

fig.label: Figure 1.

caption.p: PRISMA 2020 flow diagram for new systematic reviews which included searches of databases, registers and other sources. *Consider, if feasible to do so, reporting the number of records identified from each database or register searched (rather than the total number across all databases/registers). **If automation tools were used, indicate how many records were excluded by a human and how many were excluded by automation tools. From: Page et al. (2021).

fig.alt-text: A multi panel flowchart shows the P R I S M A 2020 flow diagram for systematic reviews.
fig.long-desc: The multi panel flowchart shows the P R I S M A 2020 flow diagram for new systematic reviews. The diagram is divided into three main sections vertically: Identification, Screening, and Included. The top section, Identification, is further divided into two columns: Identification of studies via databases on the left and Identification of studies via other methods on the right. In the Identification of studies via databases column, a box labelled Records identified from asterisk, Databases n equals 6, lists Academic Search Ultimate n equals 552, Cinahl 308, Medline 321, A P A Psych I N F O n equals 709, Scopus n equals 780, and Web of Science n equals 560. An arrow points from this box to a box labelled Records removed before screening, Duplicate records removed n equals 3100. An arrow points from this box to a box in the Screening section labelled Records screened n equals 1530. In the Identification of studies via other methods column, a box labelled Records identified from, Citation searching n equals 3, has an arrow pointing to a box in the Screening section labelled Reports sought for retrieval n equals 3. In the Screening section, the Records screened n equals 1530 box has an arrow pointing to a box labelled Records excluded by a human double asterisk n equals 1530. Another arrow points from Records screened n equals 1530 to a box labelled Reports sought for retrieval n equals 15.

sec.p: To assess the trustworthiness and quality of the selected studies, the quality critical appraisal tool of the Critical Appraisal Skills Programme (CASP) Oxford (CASP, 2018) was used. To record relevant study characteristics such as purpose, research design, sampling method, sampling and sample, and qualitative scores, the data extraction template was created. Authors I and II independently scored the articles and classified them as low or high quality (CASP scores of 0-5 and 6-10, respectively). The quality scoring of the studies showed a high degree of agreement between the two authors, with an interrater reliability of 97%. Consensus discussions resolved discrepancies. All studies were of high quality, as per CASP criteria of Long et al. (2020).

Ten qualitative studies met the inclusion criteria (Table II). One of the search terms was young adults; however, since we could not distinguish this group in all articles, we have used adults in the text. The publications were from 2005 to 2022. The participants were aged between 18 and 67 years. All the studies had a qualitative design; exploratory, retrospective, transcendental phenomenological with thematic and conceptual analysis. Five of the studies were from USA (Baker, 2005, 2006; Beebe & Sailor, 2017; Scharp & Thomas, 2017; Scharp & Thomas, 2016), four from Australia (Agllias, 2018; Bentley & Matthewson, 2020; Darlington, 2001; Verhaar et al., 2022), and one study was from Canada (Godbout & Parent, 2012). Two of the studies by Bentley and Matthewson (2020) and Verhaar et al. (2022) were recruited through global social media platforms such as Facebook and international Parental Alienation groups. The participants in these two studies were recruited internationally. All the studies reported on the results of individual interviews. Apparently, the same sample is used in the studies of Baker (2005, 2006), Scharp and McLaren (2018), and Scharp and Thomas (2016).

tr.th: Findings what are the experiences of parental alienation

tr.td: To examine the experience of adult children who initiated or maintained estrangement from one or both parents
Experienced estranged from at least one parent30–55 years

Participants often stated that they initiated or maintained estrangement to provide relief and space to heal from a difficult relationship, but it was also experienced as a considerable loss that impacted them across the lifespan. They missed having a family and the benefits of such a relationship, including emotional, financial and physical support. However, the majority stated that they did not miss ‘their own family’ or the person they were estranged from, often taking active measures to keep the estrangement status intact. Estrangement also appeared to affect participant’s experiences of other relationships with non-estranged family members, work colleagues, and potential partners and friends.
To examine empirically the long-term effects of parental alienation on adult children who were alienated from a parent as a child

Seven major areas of impact of long-term effects of parental alienation: (1) low self-esteem, (2) depression, (3) drug/alcohol abuse, (4) lack of trust, (5) alienation from own children, (6) divorcee, and (7) other.
To examine empirically the patterns of parental alienation syndrome of adults who were alienated from a parent as a child

Three distinct patterns of alienation (1) narcissistic alienating mothers in divorced families, (2) narcissistic alienating mothers in intact families, and (3) abusive/rejecting alienating mothers and fathers.Additional notable findings: (1) Alcoholism, maltreatment, and personality disorders co-occurred in most of the alienating families, (2) parental alienation occurred in intact families,(3) parental occurred in non-litigious divorced families, (4) some of the targeted parents appeared to play a role in their own alienation,and (5) the alienation was not always completely internalised.
To capture the lived experience of parental estrangement from the viewpoint of the child who is now an adult

Each participant reported themes including anger, disappointment, isolation, control, and impact. In this study, the predominantly alienating parent was the custodial parent. The results indicated that it did not matter if the noncustodial parent lived down the street or several states away; the estrangement that developed was the same for each of them.
To qualitatively investigate the lived experience of adults who were alienated from a parent during childhood as a result of the behaviours of an alienating parent
Experienced parental alienation as a child or adolescent26–54 years

Participants described experiencing abuse perpetrated by the alienating parent. They described experiencing anxiety, depression, low self-worth, guilt, attachment problems, difficulty in other relationships, and reduced or delayed educational and career attainment that they attributed to their experience of parental alienation.
To explored current relationships with parents and approaches to adult relationships among a group of young adults who had been subject children in contested custody proceedings
Experienced being subject children in contested custody proceedings18–26 years

Their current relationships with parents encompassed a range of patterns,including: continuing positive relationships with both parents, an improved relationship with a previously non-preferred parent, and an estrangedrelationship with one parent. All were determined not to repeat their parents’ pattern of divorcee and reported various strategies to achieve this, including: not rushing into relationships, developing effective relationships skills, developing a strong sense of being their own person, whether or not in a relationship, and ensuring financial security prior to marriage and having children.
To examine the life paths and lived experiences of adults who have been alienated from a parent in the past
Voluntarily rejected a parent from their life for a period of at least 6 monthsAlienated from a parent in the past24–42 years

The results suggest the presence of postseparation conflict and, in some cases, domestic violence, as well as the triangulation of the child appear to be elements that favour the emergence of parental alienation. Moreover, this study supports a multifactorial explanation of parental alienation. In the scope of lived experience, respondents associated alienation with difficulties at school, internal and external behaviour problems, and a search for identity after reaching adulthood. Finally, overcoming the state of alienation involves issues surrounding the establishment of boundaries with the alienating parent and the rebuilding of a relationship with the alienatedparent.
To explore the types of uncertainty issues and management practices of adult children who are in the estrangement process with their parents.
Voluntarily or intentionally sought distance from at least one parent20–66 years
Results reveal six types of uncertainty and six management processes. Taken together, results suggest that the estrangement process is marked by chronic uncertainty that can serve multiple functions.
To explores the ways in which the parent–child relationship is constructed with adult children who are estranged from their parent(s).
Voluntarily or intentionally sought distance from at least one parent20–66 years

Two discourses compete to illuminate meanings of the parent–child relationship.Three interrelated themes that emphasise biological connection, familial webs, and shared history constitute the Discourse of Relationship Endurance (DRE). Four interrelated themes that emphasise the need for relational maintenance, care/support, love, and individualism constitute the Discourse of Temporal Contingency and resist the DRE.
To qualitatively investigated the mental health of adults exposed to parental alienatingbehaviours in childhood.
Exposed to parental alienating behaviours ed by a parent26–59 years

Four themes were identified: mental health difficulties, including anxietydisorders and trauma reactions, emotional pain, addiction and substance use, and coping and resilience.Intergenerational transmission of parental alienation was found. Confusion in understanding their experience of alienation, the mental health sequelae, and elevated levels of suicidal ideation were found.

sec.p: Two studies aimed to describe the experience of adult children who initiated or maintained alienation from one or both parents (Agllias, 2018; Beebe & Sailor, 2017). One study explored more specifically the types of issues and management practices of adult children who are in the alienation process with their parents (Scharp & McLaren, 2018), and another how the parent-child relationship is constructed with adult children who are alienated from their parent(s) (Scharp & Thomas, 2016).

Two studies qualitatively investigated the lived experiences of adults who were alienated from a parent during childhood as a result of the behaviours of the alienating parent (Bentley & Matthewson, 2020; Godbout & Parent, 2012). One study empirically examined more specifically the long-term effects of parental alienation on adult children who were alienated from a parent as a child (Baker, 2005), and another examined the patterns of parental alienation syndrome (Baker, 2006). The mental health of adults exposed to parental alienating behaviours in childhood was explored in one study (Verhaar et al., 2022), and another analysed current relationships with parents and approaches to adult relationships among a group of young adults who had been subject to children in contested custody proceedings (Darlington, 2001).

In Phase III, reading the studies, the researchers read the selected texts to facilitate deep engagement with the material. Data extraction was conducted, and interpretive metaphors were developed to capture underlying themes. Phase IV entailed determining how the link between the different research studies, and a comparative analysis was performed. Through multiple readings, relationships among the studies were identified and clarified. In Phase V, key concepts from each study were translated across texts to identify shared meanings and divergences. See the included appendix (https://doi.org/10.18710/SY8T6Q).

Phase VI, synthesising translations, involved integrating the translations to construct a coherent synthesis of findings. Finally, in Phase VII, expressing the synthesis, the development of both analogous and refutational translations was conducted, and a new interpretation was highlighted and described as a result of the study.

In accordance with Noblit and Hare (1988), the authors were responsible for the research design, selection of phenomena, and choice of search terms and databases. The first author oversaw the selection, conduct, reading, and identification of relationships between studies, in continuous dialogue with the co-author. The translation, synthesis of translations, and presentation of the synthesis and results were carried out jointly by both authors.

sec.title: Ethical considerations
sec.p: Ethical assessment is not relevant as this is a review. However, all included studies emphasised ethical considerations.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The analysis resulted in four themes: Lack of trust and insecure relationships, Emotional pain, feelings of loss and guilt, Low self-esteem affects life paths and Embracing strategies and changes. A synthesis of the four themes is illuminated in the line of argument and metaphor: A tree that grows in the shadow of its roots.

sec.title: Lack of trust and insecure relationships
sec.p: The results show that adults who experienced parental alienation during childhood rarely disclose these experiences, except when directly asked or in conversations with individuals they deeply trust (Agllias, 2018). In adulthood, they often report persistent feelings of isolation (Beebe & Sailor, 2017), significant mental health challenges, including anxiety disorders, and ongoing difficulties in interpersonal communication (Beebe & Sailor, 2017; Verhaar et al., 2022). The rejection and mistrust internalised during childhood frequently translate into relational insecurity, making it difficult for adults to establish and maintain stable relationships (Scharp & Thomas, 2016). Estrangement influences not only relationships within the immediate family but also interactions with non-estranged relatives and broader social networks (Agllias, 2018; Bentley & Matthewson, 2020). When the alienating parent was the custodial parent, participants emphasised that geographic proximity to the noncustodial parent, whether living nearby or several states away, did not mitigate the depth of estrangement (Beebe & Sailor, 2017). Furthermore, parental divorcee was described as having a pervasive impact on adult relational patterns, affecting intimate partnerships, parenting roles, and even decisions regarding family formation. As a participant in the study by Scharp and McLaren, (2018, p. 822) explained:

p.italic: I’m often kind of amazed when I see functional families because I don’t have one. I think that I’m sure that I have less faith in my ability to have one because I didn’t see it modelled. I don’t think, I don’t want to have children. If I had a better relationship with my mother… I think I would have been more optimistic about the ability to raise children.

sec.p: Adults who experienced parental divorcee during childhood often report long-term consequences that extend well into adulthood. These include pervasive issues such as lack of trust in others (Baker, 2005), profound emotional pain (Darlington, 2001), and persistent feelings of uncertainty regarding relationships and family stability (Scharp & McLaren, 2018). In addition, both internalising and externalising behavioural difficulties have been documented, contributing to lifelong challenges in psychological well-being (Bentley & Matthewson, 2020; Darlington, 2001). Many adults reflect on the high prevalence of divorcee in society and express concern that their own family history places them at heightened risk of relational breakdown. This perceived vulnerability often shapes their attitudes toward intimacy, commitment, and family formation, reinforcing patterns of caution and anxiety in adult relationships (Agllias, 2018; Darlington, 2001).

sec.title: Emotional pain, feelings of loss and guilt
sec.p: The results show that adults who experienced parental alienation during childhood often describe initiating or maintaining estrangement as a deliberate strategy to create emotional distance and allow space for healing from a difficult relationship. While this decision can provide temporary relief, participants consistently emphasised that alienation also represents a profound and enduring loss, with implications that extend across the lifespan (Agllias, 2018; Baker, 2006). Feelings of grief and trauma were reported regardless of who initiated the estrangement or the perceived benefits of separation (Agllias, 2018). Importantly, alienation was not always fully internalised, and some adults continued to struggle with unresolved emotions and identity conflicts (Baker, 2006).

Parental alienation occurred in intact families as well as in non-litigious families (Baker, 2006), and the adults expressed that they had missed having a family and the benefits of such a relationship, including emotional, financial, and physical support (Agllias, 2018). In adulthood, they ruminated about what had happened, why it had happened, and why the parents had rejected and picked them, and they experienced it as a betrayal (Agllias, 2018). However, experiences were not uniform. While some adults actively sought to maintain estrangement to protect themselves, others explored pathways toward reconciliation, often involving the establishment of boundaries with the alienating parent and efforts to rebuild connections with the alienated parent (Agllias, 2018; Godbout & Parent, 2012). These processes were described as complex and multifactorial, reflecting the interplay of psychological, relational, and contextual factors (Baker, 2006; Godbout & Parent, 2012).

The results show that parental alienating behaviours can have a profound impact on mental health later in life. Adults reported a wide range of mental health difficulties, including anger, anxiety, emotional pain, guilt, isolation, depression, and suicidal ideation, as well as addiction and substance use. Some of these conditions were clinically diagnosed, while others were self-identified (Baker, 2005; Verhaar et al., 2022). Anger was particularly salient during the development of estrangement, with some participants expressing hostility toward both parents for placing them in the middle of conflict, which contributed to emotional and psychological distancing from their parents (Beebe & Sailor, 2017; Bentley & Matthewson, 2020). Anxiety persisted into adulthood, often interfering with daily functioning and relational stability (Verhaar et al., 2022).

Adults were acutely aware of the long-term ramifications of parental alienation during childhood on their identity and life trajectory. Reported challenges included low self-esteem, personality disorders, addiction, relational breakdowns, and alienation from their own children, underscoring the intergenerational impact of these experiences (Agllias, 2018; Baker, 2005; Bentley & Matthewson, 2020; Darlington, 2001; Godbout & Parent, 2012; Scharp & McLaren, 2018; Scharp & Thomas, 2016; Verhaar et al., 2022).

sec.title: Low self-esteem affects life paths
sec.p: The results highlight that adults who experienced parental alienation during childhood frequently report markedly low levels of self-esteem, a theme consistently highlighted across multiple studies (Agllias, 2018; Baker, 2005; Bentley & Matthewson, 2020; Godbout & Parent, 2012; Scharp & McLaren, 2018; Verhaar et al., 2022). One significant contributing factor is the internalisation of negative messages directed toward the targeted parent, which participants often interpret as a rejection of the part of themselves that resembles that parent. This dynamic creates a profound identity conflict, as the alienating parent’s hostility toward the other parent is perceived as a denial of the child’s own essence (Bentley & Matthewson, 2020). As illustrated by participants in Baker (2005, p. 294) study, repeated exposure to statements portraying the targeted parent as fundamentally flawed or dangerous led many to question their own worth:

p.italic: When you have somebody like my mother who is constantly sitting there telling you this person who is your dad and is a part of you is such a bad person, and he is going to do all these terrible things and it is like if he is so bad and I am part of him, then doesn’t that sort of make me like that too?

sec.p: Such internalised narratives were particularly impactful for those alienated from a same-sex parent, as physical resemblance intensified feelings of shame and self-rejection over time. Adults described struggling with low confidence in their abilities, diminished belief in their prospects, and a pervasive sense of inadequacy, which often shaped their educational and career trajectories. Participants expressed having low confidence in themselves and their abilities. As a participant in the study by Bentley & Matthewson, (2020, p. 516) explained:

p.italic: I never had any self-esteem, I never believed in myself. And I spent too many years
trying to put myself back together rather than create a future for myself. Because I
didn’t think I actually had a future.

sec.p: Adults often associate their educational challenges with the disruptions caused by parental divorcee (Godbout & Parent, 2012). They describe encountering significant obstacles to learning that shaped their educational trajectories and, ultimately, their life paths. Many reported challenges in maintaining focus and achieving academic success, attributing these struggles to anxiety, ongoing conflict, and the stress of family instability. (Bentley & Matthewson, 2020). As a participant in the study by Bentley and Matthewson, (2020, p. 517) explained:

p.italic: “Well I actually sat my exams, I ran away from home…and things got so bad…I thought I’d had enough, and so…I went and I packed a bag, and I ended up finding.

like a local field that was near my high school. So, I was going to school, for like study practice, cause this was like the 2 weeks leading up to my final exams, and I was revising under like a tarp…”

sec.p: Adults emphasised that they felt that they were hindered from fulfilling their potential at school because the needs of their parents were prioritised over their own. Some, in addition, referred to difficulties with employment and further education in some situations, affirming that their mental health was an ongoing barrier to their career (Bentley & Matthewson, 2020).

sec.title: Embracing strategies and changes
sec.p: The results showed that adults who experienced parental alienation during childhood often demonstrate a strong determination not to replicate the dysfunctional patterns observed in their families of origin. Many report adopting deliberate strategies aimed at fostering stability and relational health in adulthood. (Beebe & Sailor, 2017) These strategies include delaying commitment, carefully selecting a life partner, and prioritising efforts to maintain positive and enduring relationships. A recurring theme, according to Darlington (2001), is the development of a robust sense of individuality and autonomy, ensuring that their identity remains intact regardless of relationship status. Participants also emphasised the importance of financial security prior to marriage and parenthood, viewing economic stability as a safeguard against relational breakdown. Furthermore, effective communication was consistently identified as a cornerstone of healthy relationships, reflecting a conscious effort to counteract the secrecy and conflict that characterised their childhood experiences. As a participant in the study by Darlington (2001, p. 40) explained:

p.italic: I knew that whatever happened, I would not want to put my kid through what I went through... I would take every step and means to make my marriage work and to find somebody that I knew I could live with the rest of my life before I got married… And then when I met my husband, his own family went through some pretty horrific times… So both of us together had this goal that we would never put our kids through what we went through, and we discussed it at length… We both told each other and made a definite goal that we wouldn’t do that, and if we were going to get married, it was going to be forever. And to work out our problems before they get to the stage that our parents did.

sec.title: Line of argument
sec.p: By reflecting on the themes generated, a lines-of-argument synthesis (Noblit & Hare, 1988). The results are linked to the metaphor: A tree that grows in the shadow of its roots.

A tree growing in uncertain conditions offers a powerful metaphor for understanding the experiences of adults affected by parental alienation. When water is scarce, the tree struggles to develop strong roots and a stable structure. Similarly, adults who grew up in insecure relational environments often face challenges in establishing trust and security, reflecting the theme of lack of trust and insecure relationships. The roots symbolise early attachment experiences, caregiving quality, and the fulfilment, or lack, of basic emotional needs, which determine the tree’s stability and capacity for growth, just as early relational security shapes an individual’s psychological resilience. The branches represent later-life stressors and relational challenges. When a branch breaks during a storm, the tree suffers a structural loss, leaving scars even after healing. In the same way, adults experience profound emotional pain, feelings of loss, and guilt when alienated from a parent or significant relationship. These emotional wounds may persist across the lifespan, shaping identity and relational patterns. A tree growing in the shadow of others receives limited sunlight and cannot reach its full potential. This mirrors the experience of adults with low self-esteem, who often feel inadequate and constrained in their ability to achieve personal and professional goals. Internalised negative messages and identity conflicts further hinder growth and confidence. Yet, just as a tree adapts by seeking new sources of nourishment, adults can develop strategies to overcome these challenges. Through conscious efforts, such as building healthy relationships, prioritising communication, and establishing boundaries, they embrace change and resilience, aligning with the theme of embracing strategies and changes. These adaptive responses illustrate that while early experiences cast a long shadow, growth and transformation remain possible.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The results of this study show that parental alienation is a complex phenomenon with various complex choices that can have profound effects on adulthood and further life. On the one hand, parental alienation and estrangement gained relief and space to heal from difficult relationships, and they took active measures to keep the estrangement intact. At the same time, the choice led to the experience of loss and grief. Galvin and Todres (2013) emphasise that grief can have a profound impact on a person's well-being and health. Grief can affect both physical and mental health, and it is important to recognise and understand this impact to provide support in an empathetic and humane manner. It is central to acknowledge the individual's unique experiences and emotions and thereby alleviate suffering and promote a sense of well-being.

Adults missed the support and benefits of family, and the estrangement affected them throughout their lives. (cf. Agllias, 2018) The results highlight experiences of lifelong consequences for their well-being, including behavioural problems and emotional pain. The uncertainty and emotional pain that accompany alienation could lead to difficulties in managing emotions and building trusting and healthy relationships in adulthood. (cf. Darlington, 2001; Scharp & McLaren, 2018) Despite the challenges, the results highlight that adults were determined not to repeat their parents' patterns and emphasised the importance of not rushing into relationships, choosing their life partner carefully, and maintaining good relationships and communication skills. (cf. Darlington, 2001).

The results highlight descriptions of expressed low self-esteem, confidence in oneself, and one's abilities. The self-image and identity of adults who have experienced parental alienation in their childhood can lead to feelings of inadequacy and low self-esteem. Constantly questioning ones own worth and place in the family can create an uncertain identity and difficulties to find the own path in life. (cf. Bentley & Matthewson, 2020) Galvin and Todres (2013) emphasise that poor self-confidence can limit a person's ability to exercise their freedom and influence their situation. By acknowledging and understanding these limitations, individuals and carers can work together to strengthen self-confidence, thereby improving the individual's well-being and agency.

The results confirm previous research and the assumption that experiencing parents’ divorcee often suggests living in an estrangement process marked by uncertainty. (Gerrand & Nduna, 2021; Lundberg, 2017; Meland et al., 2023; Reuven‐Krispin et al., 2021) The estrangement process is wrought with long-lasting and cyclical uncertainties for those initiating the distance. (cf. Sharp & McLaren, 2017). However, these uncertainties are not necessarily problematic. In fact, from time to time, adults sought to maintain the feeling of uncertainty to keep a distance from an unhealthy relationship with their parents. Uncertainty can be tied to vulnerability. Carel (2009), cf. Røysland et al. (2023) claims that vulnerability is specifically related to a human being’s embodied state, which marks the distinction between objective and subjective vulnerability. By avoiding close relationships, people are exposed to what is described as objective vulnerability. The response to illness varies, and there are no crucial links between what Carel (2009) describes as illness and the feeling of subjective vulnerability. In line with Dahlberg and Segesten (2010), health occurs in a balanced feeling of vulnerability and freedom, and well-being and capability are to follow the own life projects. According to Galvin and Todres (2013), freedom is not absolute but always situated within certain limitations. Our social, cultural, and material circumstances affect how we can exercise our freedom. Despite these limitations, we still have a certain degree of agency that allows us to influence and change our situation and create new opportunities for ourselves. As humans, we are vulnerable and have our limitations. Recognising our vulnerability is important for understanding the real conditions of our freedom.

Some of the adults who had experienced parental divorcee during childhood claimed to have learned new skills for communication, and they had experienced being more capable after experiencing parental divorcee. Wilde (1999) talks about the skilful body and how a person who has challenging experiences from childhood is forced to learn new skills and relearn lost skills. They are bending towards the light and alternative sources of nourishment. To use the metaphor in the study, adults learn to adapt, but the shadow of the tree is a reminder of the consequences of parental alienation. This knowledge is needed to support growth and capability. By understanding the experiences of parental alienation, we can better support adults affected by parental alienation and help them to strengthen their roots, their self-esteem, relationships, and a healthy and meaningful life. Supporting individuals in embracing a balanced sense of vulnerability and freedom contributes to health and well-being and the ability to follow one's life path.

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: A strength of this meta-ethnography lies in its methodological rigour, particularly the comprehensive literature search conducted across six electronic databases. From a total of 1,530 articles, only 10 met the predefined inclusion criteria, underscoring the limited availability of qualitative research addressing parental alienation and its influence on adult identity and well-being. The application of the CASP (Critical Appraisal Skills Programme) quality appraisal tool, alongside adherence to Noblit and Hare (1988) meta-ethnographic framework and the eMERGe reporting guidelines (France et al., 2019), enhanced the reflexivity of the research process. The eMERGe guidelines, comprising 19 reporting criteria spanning all phases of the meta-ethnographic method, contributed significantly to the transparency and trustworthiness of the study. Reflexivity was operationalized by documenting assumptions, questioning interpretive decisions, and considering alternative explanations during synthesis. This approach, combined with adherence to Noblit and Hare’s!988) framework and eMERGe guidelines, enhanced transparency and trustworthiness. A strength of this meta-ethnography lies not only in its methodological rigour but also in the reflexive stance adopted throughout the research process. The author’s experiential background in family and relational studies informed the interpretation of findings, yet this influence was critically examined through analytic memos and collaborative discussions. An additional strength is the diverse experiential background of the author, encompassing both contextual and methodological expertise. This multidimensional perspective enriched the analytical process and provided a robust foundation for interpreting the complex phenomenon under investigation.

A limitation of this study lies in the challenge of locating qualitative research that offers firsthand experience of parental alienation and its impact on adult identity and well-being. The apparent reuse of participant samples across included studies may compromise the validity of the synthesis by limiting the diversity of perspectives represented.

Additionally, several records failed to meet the applied search criteria, raising the possibility that relevant studies may have been inadvertently excluded due to limitations in the search terminology. Nevertheless, it is important to emphasise that multiple search terms were systematically tested prior to finalising the strategy, and these alternatives did not yield additional eligible records beyond those identified through the selected terms.

To enhance the robustness of the search process, an academic librarian was consulted throughout the development of the strategy, ensuring that the most effective and comprehensive approach was employed.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Parental alienation during childhood can affect relationships, including intimate relationships and parenting, and contribute to emotional pain, uncertainty, behavioural problems, and long-term psychological effects in adulthood. The findings highlight difficulties in maintaining relationships due to early experiences of rejection and mistrust. Parental alienation can also lead to lifelong feelings of loss and grief. Those who lack emotional and practical support from their family during the time of divorcee may feel betrayed and often struggle with unresolved questions. Parental alienation impacts self-esteem and the ability to reach full potential in adulthood. Despite the challenges, many adults strive to break negative patterns. They adopt strategies such as choosing their life partners carefully, prioritising financial stability, and emphasising good communication. Building a strong personal identity and avoiding rushed relationships are central themes in their efforts to create a healthier life. The findings may help to identify and address areas in need of improvement for family care workers, health professionals, and educators who care for adults who struggle with the consequences later in life. The study’s results are also important in terms of health promotion measures for children and adolescents who experience parental alienation during their childhood. The results of this study highlight learned skills, strengths, and capabilities. Further research could be conducted to gain deeper knowledge about which skills or coping strategies parental alienation during childhood has provided for adulthood.

bio.p: Ingrid Ølfarnes Røysland, RPN, MSc, PhD, is an associate professor at the Department of Public Health, Faculty of Health Sciences at the University of Stavanger, Stavanger, Norway.
Camilla Ann-Louise Koskinen, RN, PhD, is a professor at the Department of Care and Ethics, Faculty of Health Sciences University of Stavanger, N-4036 Stavanger, Norway.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: Authors have no competing interests to report.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This research received no specific grant from any funding agency in the public, commercial or not-for-profit sectors.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: All data generated or analysed during this study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

sec.title: Ethics approval statement
sec.p: As this review involves material previously published or in the public domain, ethical approval is not required.

sec.title: Consent for publication
sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Supplemental material
sec.p: Supplemental data for this article can be accessed at https://doi.org/10.1080/17482631.2026.2647083.
